This extends the recently developed entanglement principle for the fractional Laplacian on $\mathbb R^n$ to the negatively curved setting of hyperbolic space. As an application, we derive global uniqueness results for inverse problems associated with fractional polyharmonic equations on $\mathbb H^n$, including a fractional Calderón problem. The proof relies on the heat semigroup representation of fractional powers together with sharp global heat kernel estimates on hyperbolic space.

AI.tldr: Extends entanglement principle from Euclidean to hyperbolic space for fractional Laplacians, enabling global uniqueness results for inverse problems.
AI.motivation: To generalize the recently developed entanglement principle for fractional Laplacians from Euclidean space ℝⁿ to negatively curved hyperbolic space ℍⁿ, establishing analogous properties in non-Euclidean geometry.
AI.method: Uses heat semigroup representation of fractional powers combined with sharp global heat kernel estimates on hyperbolic space to prove that if finitely many distinct noninteger powers of -Δ_ℍⁿ vanish on a common open set, they must vanish identically.
AI.result: Establishes entanglement principle for fractional powers of Laplace-Beltrami operator on hyperbolic space, proving that linear dependence on a common open set implies global vanishing. Derives global uniqueness results for inverse problems including fractional Calderón problem.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extends entanglement principle to hyperbolic space, providing new tools for studying inverse problems associated with fractional polyharmonic equations in negatively curved geometries.

title: Infrared Thermography in the Tokamak à Configuration Variable
summary: In the Tokamak à Configuration Variable (TCV), infrared thermography (IR) is currently composed of the horizontal, vertical, and tangential infrared systems (HIR, VIR, TIR), which all use Equus 81k M cameras. The IR diagnostics obtain the surface temperature of TCV's graphite tiles for post-discharge analysis. Target heat flux profiles are inferred from the tile temperature with the THEODOR (Thermal Energy Onto Divertor) code. Fast transient analysis is possible in reduced frame mode, with acquisition frequencies above 10kHz. The main views are the lower inner wall for HIR, the floor for VIR, and the lower outer wall for TIR. The HIR camera can also be moved to view the midplane inner wall, while TIR can be moved to see the midplane inner wall and the upper outer wall, mainly to measure synchrotron radiation and heat deposition due to runaway electrons. Recent developments in TCV's IR systems include (i) tile diffusivity and conductivity measurements to assure the precision of heat flux estimates; (ii) the addition of one new VIR heated valley tile and two rooftop TIR tiles, for measurements of fast heat flux transients; (iii) the implementation of long-pass wavelength filter of 4095 nm, to diminish the measurement of plasma parasitic infrared light, mainly from deuterium 5-4 emission at 4051 nm. Despite these developments, the main sources of uncertainty for IR in TCV are still parasitic infrared light and the determination of the surface layer heat transmission factor, both of which mainly affect the VIR system.

AI.tldr: TCV's infrared thermography systems (HIR, VIR, TIR) measure graphite tile temperatures for heat flux analysis, with recent improvements in tile characterization, additional measurement points, and parasitic light filtering, though uncertainties remain.
AI.motivation: To monitor and analyze heat deposition on TCV's graphite tiles during plasma operations, enabling post-discharge heat flux analysis and understanding of plasma-wall interactions.
AI.method: Uses three infrared systems (HIR, VIR, TIR) with Equus 81k M cameras to measure surface temperatures. Heat flux is inferred using THEODOR code. Recent developments include tile diffusivity/conductivity measurements, additional heated valley and rooftop tiles, and 4095 nm long-pass filters to reduce parasitic infrared light.
AI.result: The IR systems provide temperature measurements for heat flux analysis with fast transient capabilities (>10kHz). Recent improvements enhance measurement precision and reduce parasitic light interference, though uncertainties from parasitic infrared light and surface layer heat transmission factors persist, particularly affecting the VIR system.
AI.conclusion: TCV's infrared thermography systems have been enhanced with new capabilities and filtering techniques, but key uncertainties remain that primarily affect the vertical infrared system, highlighting areas for future improvement in heat flux measurement accuracy.

title: Debye Relaxation in Model-Based Multi-Dimensional Magnetic Particle Imaging
summary: Model-based reconstruction approaches for the medical imaging modality Magnetic Particle Imaging (MPI) are typically based on the Langevin model, which assumes instantaneous alignment of the particles magnetic momenta with the applied field. Regarding the application to real data, Langevin model-based reconstruction methods require model transfer functions (MTF) obtained from calibrations to preprocess the data. There are also model-based reconstruction approaches that include relaxation effects and other particle-level dynamics. However, they are limited either to 1D or 1D-like scanning scenarios when considering real data, or are limited to simulated data in the case of multi-dimensional field-free point (FFP) MPI. Thus, fully model-based reconstructions from multi-dimensional FFP scanning data that incorporate relaxation effects without using an MTF have not yet been demonstrated. In this work, we incorporate relaxation effects by considering a multi-dimensional Debye model and provide reconstruction formulae. In particular, we show that the Debye model-based signal is the response of a linear time-invariant system with exponential memory applied to a Langevin model-based signal. We provide a reconstruction algorithm for the introduced multi-dimensional Debye model. To this end, we devise a relaxation adaption step. For the resulting relaxation-adapted Debye signal, we show that it can be expressed by the well-studied MPI core operator derived from the Langevin theory. This results in a three-stage algorithm with low additional cost over the Langevin model, as the relaxation adaption scales linearly in the input data. We provide numerical results for the proposed algorithmic approach. In particular, we obtain fully model-based reconstructions from real 2D MPI data without involving any specific MTF analogous to the Langevin model case.

AI.tldr: Proposes a multi-dimensional Debye model for MPI reconstruction that incorporates relaxation effects without requiring model transfer functions, enabling fully model-based reconstructions from real 2D MPI data.
AI.motivation: Current MPI reconstruction methods either use Langevin model with MTF calibrations (limited by instantaneous alignment assumption) or relaxation models limited to 1D/1D-like scenarios or simulations. No fully model-based reconstructions exist for multi-dimensional FFP MPI that incorporate relaxation without MTF.
AI.method: Introduces multi-dimensional Debye model incorporating relaxation effects, shows it's a linear time-invariant system with exponential memory applied to Langevin signal. Develops reconstruction algorithm with relaxation adaption step, resulting in three-stage algorithm that scales linearly with input data.
AI.result: Achieves fully model-based reconstructions from real 2D MPI data without using specific MTF, analogous to Langevin model case. The relaxation-adapted Debye signal can be expressed using existing MPI core operator from Langevin theory.
AI.conclusion: Successfully demonstrates first fully model-based reconstruction for multi-dimensional FFP MPI incorporating relaxation effects without MTF, with low computational overhead compared to Langevin model.

title: Second order classification for singular Liouville equations with a coefficient function
summary: In this article we are concerned with the existence of blow-up solutions to the following boundary value problem $$-Δv= λV(x) |x|^2e^v\;\mbox{in}\quad B_1,\quad v=0 \;\mbox{ on }\quad \partial B_1,$$ where $B_1$ is the unit ball in $\mathbb R^2$ centered at the origin, $V(x)$ is a positive smooth potential, and $λ>0$ is a small parameter. We find necessary and sufficient conditions on the potential $V$ for the existence of a blow-up sequence of solutions tending to infinity near the origin as $λ\to 0^+$. In particular, we obtain a second-order classification of the coefficient function $V$ for which (simple) blow-up occurs at the origin.

AI.tldr: The paper studies existence of blow-up solutions to a semilinear elliptic equation with exponential nonlinearity in 2D, finding necessary and sufficient conditions on the potential V for blow-up behavior as λ→0⁺.
AI.motivation: To understand when blow-up solutions occur for semilinear elliptic equations with exponential nonlinearities in bounded domains, particularly how the potential function V influences blow-up behavior near singular points.
AI.method: Analysis of the boundary value problem -Δv = λV(x)|x|²eᵛ in the unit ball B₁ with Dirichlet boundary conditions, studying asymptotic behavior as λ→0⁺ and classifying the potential V through second-order conditions.
AI.result: Found necessary and sufficient conditions on the positive smooth potential V for existence of blow-up solutions that tend to infinity near the origin as λ→0⁺, including second-order classification for simple blow-up at the origin.
AI.conclusion: The potential V plays a crucial role in determining blow-up behavior, with specific conditions governing whether blow-up occurs at the origin as the parameter λ approaches zero.

title: Optimization of stellarator configurations combining omnigenity and piecewise omnigenity
summary: We present a method for optimizing stellarator configurations that combine omnigenity and piecewise omnigenity (pwO). Within the \texttt{OOPS} optimization framework [Liu \textit{et al.}, arXiv:2502.09350 (2025)], we introduce a mapping technique that can ``squeeze'' general omnigenous fields to approximate pwO in the high-field side. Using this approach, we obtain a range of optimized configurations that combine poloidal omnigenity (PO) and pwO, spanning different field periods and aspect ratios. We further show that these configurations are compatible with a magnetic well. The resulting configurations exhibit favorable neoclassical transport and bootstrap current properties while partially relaxing the strict constraints of omnigenity. These results suggest that such configurations are promising candidates for future stellarator reactors.

AI.tldr: A method for optimizing stellarator configurations that combine omnigenity and piecewise omnigenity, achieving favorable neoclassical transport and bootstrap current properties while relaxing strict omnigenity constraints.
AI.motivation: To develop stellarator configurations that maintain good neoclassical transport properties while relaxing the strict constraints of full omnigenity, making them more practical for reactor designs.
AI.method: Within the OOPS optimization framework, introduced a mapping technique to "squeeze" general omnigenous fields to approximate piecewise omnigenity on the high-field side, optimizing configurations that combine poloidal omnigenity and piecewise omnigenity.
AI.result: Obtained optimized configurations spanning different field periods and aspect ratios that are compatible with magnetic wells, exhibiting favorable neoclassical transport and bootstrap current properties.
AI.conclusion: Configurations combining omnigenity and piecewise omnigenity are promising candidates for future stellarator reactors, offering good transport properties while relaxing strict omnigenity constraints.

title: Bathymetry reconstruction via optimal control in well-balanced finite element methods for the shallow water equations
summary: Accurate prediction of shallow water flows relies on precise bottom topography data, yet direct bathymetric surveys are expensive and time-consuming. In contrast, remote sensing platforms such as radar or satellite altimetry provide accurate free surface observations. This disparity motivates a data-driven reconstruction strategy: invert the shallow water equations to estimate the bathymetry that yields the best fit to the governing dynamics. We introduce a new direct reconstruction technique that extracts bathymetric features from widely available free surface measurements. The underlying inverse problem of determining an unknown bathymetry profile from observed wave elevations is inherently ill-posed. Small perturbations in the data may lead to large deviations in the reconstructed topography, and discontinuities or sharp gradients further exacerbate instability. To stabilize the inversion, we formulate an optimal-control problem, wherein a cost functional penalizes deviations between simulated and measured free surface elevation while enforcing a state equation for the flow dynamics. To suppress noise and preserve sharp depth variations, the framework is augmented with $L^1$ regularization and total variation denoising. These sparsity-promoting terms encourage piecewise-smooth solutions, allowing changes in the bathymetry to be captured without excessive smoothing. Numerical experiments on synthetic noisy data and discontinuous bathymetry demonstrate robust performance in reconstructing unknown bathymetry.

AI.tldr: A data-driven method to reconstruct bathymetry from free surface measurements by inverting shallow water equations with L1 and total variation regularization for stability.
AI.motivation: Direct bathymetric surveys are expensive and time-consuming, while remote sensing provides accurate free surface observations. This disparity motivates using free surface measurements to reconstruct bottom topography through inverse modeling.
AI.method: Formulate an optimal-control problem that penalizes deviations between simulated and measured free surface elevation while enforcing shallow water dynamics. Augment with L1 regularization and total variation denoising to suppress noise and preserve sharp depth variations, encouraging piecewise-smooth solutions.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on synthetic noisy data and discontinuous bathymetry demonstrate robust performance in reconstructing unknown bathymetry, showing the method can handle ill-posed inverse problems with data perturbations and sharp gradients.
AI.conclusion: The proposed direct reconstruction technique successfully extracts bathymetric features from free surface measurements by combining optimal control with sparsity-promoting regularization, providing a practical alternative to expensive direct surveys.

title: Sheafs of ultradifferentiable functions
summary: An abstract theory of ultradifferentiable sheafs is developed. Moreover, various applications to the theory of linear partial differential equations, differential geometry and, in particular, CR geometry are discussed.

AI.tldr: Develops abstract theory of ultradifferentiable sheaves with applications to PDEs, differential geometry, and CR geometry.
AI.motivation: To establish a comprehensive abstract framework for ultradifferentiable sheaves that can be applied across multiple mathematical domains including partial differential equations and geometry.
AI.method: Develops an abstract theory of ultradifferentiable sheaves, building formal mathematical structures and properties for these sheaves.
AI.result: Creates a foundational theory of ultradifferentiable sheaves that enables applications to linear PDEs, differential geometry, and CR geometry.
AI.conclusion: The abstract theory provides a powerful framework that unifies and advances research in multiple areas of mathematics through ultradifferentiable sheaf techniques.

title: Intrinsic Nonlocality of Spin- and Polarization-Resolved Probabilities in Strong-Field Quantum Electrodynamics
summary: Spin and polarization are central to precision tests of fundamental physics and for interpreting radiation from astrophysical sources and ultraintense laser-matter experiments. Predictive modeling therefore requires not only energy spectra, but also angle-, spin-, and polarization-resolved particle distributions. Here, we demonstrate that a key assumption underlying current strong-field quantum electrodynamics (QED) models, i.e., that emission can be treated as an instantaneous random event sampled from a local differential rate, breaks down once emission angles, electron spin, and/or photon polarization are resolved. Namely, the resulting fully differential distribution can deviate strongly from the true result and can even yield inconsistent probabilities that take negative values. The physical reason is simple: a photon emission probability builds up over a finite length of the electron trajectory, the formation region, during which the electron direction changes by roughly the same small angle that defines the radiation cone. We therefore integrate over this formation region analytically to obtain a physically consistent electron spin and photon polarization model whose implementation is compatible with existing Monte Carlo and particle-in-cell (PIC) workflows. Simulations of a GeV-class electron-laser collision and of emission in a pulsar-like magnetic field reveal spin and polarization patterns that differ even qualitatively from state-of-the-art local models. In particular, our new model predicts substantial angle-dependent circular photon polarization where the standard approach yields none, and a pronounced helicity bias in the recoiling electrons absent from current predictions. These findings have direct implications for upcoming strong-field QED experiments and for interpreting polarized radiation from extreme astrophysical environments.

AI.tldr: Current strong-field QED models break down when resolving emission angles, electron spin, and photon polarization, leading to inconsistent probabilities. A new formation-region-integrated model fixes this and reveals qualitatively different spin/polarization patterns.
AI.motivation: Spin and polarization are crucial for precision physics tests and interpreting radiation from astrophysical sources and intense laser experiments. Current models fail when resolving these degrees of freedom, producing inconsistent results including negative probabilities.
AI.method: The authors analytically integrate over the formation region (finite trajectory length where emission probability builds up) to create a physically consistent electron spin and photon polarization model. This approach is compatible with existing Monte Carlo and particle-in-cell workflows.
AI.result: Simulations show the new model produces qualitatively different spin and polarization patterns compared to standard local models: substantial angle-dependent circular photon polarization (absent in standard approach) and pronounced helicity bias in recoiling electrons.
AI.conclusion: The formation-region-integrated model resolves fundamental issues in strong-field QED when resolving spin/polarization degrees of freedom. These findings have direct implications for upcoming strong-field QED experiments and interpreting polarized radiation from extreme astrophysical environments.

title: An error control framework for computing the exponential of matrices arising from the finite element discretization
summary: Several methods for computing the action of the matrix exponential $\mathrm{e}^{\boldsymbol{A}} \boldsymbol{b}$ are expressed by substituting $\boldsymbol{A}$ into a rational approximation of the scalar exponential function. The error of such methods can be estimated using the numerical range of $\boldsymbol{A}$, which enables the computation of $\mathrm{e}^{\boldsymbol{A}}\boldsymbol{b}$ with a prescribed accuracy. However, when the input matrix has the structure $\boldsymbol{A} = τ\boldsymbol{M}^{-1} \boldsymbol{K}$, this approach is challenging because computing the bounding box of numerical range is difficult and the numerical range may be too large to construct rational approximations on it. In this paper, focusing on the case where $\boldsymbol{M}$ is a well-conditioned symmetric positive definite matrix, we propose considering the numerical range of a similarity transformed matrix of $\boldsymbol{A}$. The numerical range of transformed matrix is not only numerically computable but can also be theoretically bounded depending on properties of $\boldsymbol{K}$. Numerical experiments confirm that the computations can be performed within the prescribed error tolerance.

AI.tldr: Proposes using similarity transformation to compute numerical range for matrix exponential e^A b when A = τM^{-1}K, enabling error-controlled computation with prescribed accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for computing matrix exponential e^A b using rational approximations rely on numerical range of A for error estimation. However, when A = τM^{-1}K, computing numerical range is difficult and may be too large for effective rational approximations.
AI.method: Focuses on case where M is well-conditioned symmetric positive definite matrix. Proposes considering numerical range of similarity transformed matrix of A instead of original A. This transformed numerical range is both numerically computable and theoretically bounded based on properties of K.
AI.result: Numerical experiments confirm that computations can be performed within prescribed error tolerance using the proposed approach with similarity transformed numerical range.
AI.conclusion: The similarity transformation approach enables practical error-controlled computation of matrix exponential e^A b for structured matrices A = τM^{-1}K, overcoming limitations of direct numerical range computation.

title: Global-in-time strong solutions for the 2D and 3D generalized compressible Navier-Stokes-Korteweg system with arbitrarily large initial data
summary: In 1901, Korteweg formulated a constitutive equation for the Cauchy stress tensor to provide a continuum mechanical model for capillarity within fluids. Dunn and Serrin [Arch. Ration. Mech. Anal. 88(2):95-133,1985] in 1985 further modified the system of compressible fluids based on the Korteweg theory of capillarity. Since then, for the 2D and 3D compressible Navier-Stokes-Korteweg system, the global existence of strong solutions with arbitrarily large initial data have remained a challenging open problem. In this paper, we provide an affirmative answer to this longstanding open problem. Specifically, under the assumption that the viscosity coefficients satisfy a BD-type algebraic relation of the form $μ(ρ)=νρ^α$ and $λ(ρ)=2ν(α-1)ρ^α$, and that the Korteweg stress tensor complies with a generalized Bohm identity of the form $κ(ρ)=\varepsilon^2α^2ρ^{2α-3}$, we establish the global existence of strong solutions for the 2D and 3D systems in torus with arbitrarily large regular initial data. The analysis is carried out in the intermediary non-dispersive regime, characterized by the condition that the capillarity coefficient constant $\varepsilon$ does not exceed the viscosity constant $ν$. This result provides the first proof of the global-in-time existence of strong solutions for the 3D general Navier-Stokes-Korteweg system with arbitrarily large initial data in the non-dispersive regime.

AI.tldr: Global existence of strong solutions for 2D/3D compressible Navier-Stokes-Korteweg system with arbitrarily large initial data in non-dispersive regime.
AI.motivation: The global existence of strong solutions for compressible Navier-Stokes-Korteweg system with arbitrarily large initial data has been a longstanding open problem since Korteweg's 1901 capillarity model and Dunn & Serrin's 1985 modifications.
AI.method: Assume viscosity coefficients satisfy BD-type relation μ(ρ)=νρ^α, λ(ρ)=2ν(α-1)ρ^α, and Korteweg stress tensor follows generalized Bohm identity κ(ρ)=ε^2α^2ρ^{2α-3}. Analysis conducted in non-dispersive regime where capillarity coefficient ε ≤ viscosity constant ν.
AI.result: First proof of global-in-time existence of strong solutions for 3D general Navier-Stokes-Korteweg system with arbitrarily large initial data in non-dispersive regime, also establishing same for 2D system.
AI.conclusion: Provides affirmative answer to longstanding open problem, establishing global existence of strong solutions for compressible Navier-Stokes-Korteweg systems with arbitrarily large initial data under specific coefficient relations in non-dispersive regime.

title: Strong Prevalence of Hammerhead Velocity Distributions Close to the Heliospheric Current Sheet
comment: Main text: 9 pages, 4 figures. Appendix: 4 pages, 5 figures, 1 table. Submitted to Astrophysical Journal Letters
summary: The solar wind undergoes non-adiabatic heating as it travels away from the Sun. The velocity phase space distribution of non-equilibrium ions in the solar wind indicate a source of free energy that could contribute significantly to this heating. Parker Solar Probe (PSP) has observed velocity distributions containing highly anisotropic, perpendicularly diffused proton beams with a distinctly constricted gap between the core and beam populations. These distributions resemble a ``hammerhead" shape and were first reported in the fourth PSP encounter. Numerical simulations have reproduced the qualitative nature of hammerheads under certain initial conditions, but have not convincingly captured the prevalence or extreme attributes of the observed beam. This necessitates a broad study of the occurrence conditions and the associated plasma processes to better guide simulations. We statistically investigate the occurrence of these structures from 20 recent PSP encounters, and find that hammerheads dominantly occur around the Heliospheric Current Sheet (HCS). As the inclination of the HCS at PSP crossing points increases over the rising phase of the solar cycle, the occurrence of hammerheads is increasingly concentrated in narrow time periods around the HCS crossings. For comparison with previous work, we present statistical trends in the anisotropy of the proton beam and its connection to the density of proton beams as well as the drift speed of the beam to the core. Our study establishes a consistent occurrence pattern of hammerhead distributions around the HCS indicating hammerheads are diagnostics of energization processes associated with the HCS and its escaping wind.

AI.tldr: Hammerhead proton distributions in solar wind are strongly associated with Heliospheric Current Sheet crossings, indicating they're diagnostics of HCS-related energization processes.
AI.motivation: Previous simulations haven't fully captured the prevalence and extreme attributes of observed hammerhead proton distributions in solar wind, necessitating a broader study of their occurrence conditions and associated plasma processes.
AI.method: Statistical investigation of hammerhead distributions from 20 recent Parker Solar Probe encounters, analyzing their occurrence patterns relative to the Heliospheric Current Sheet and solar cycle phase.
AI.result: Hammerheads dominantly occur around the Heliospheric Current Sheet (HCS), with increasing concentration in narrow time periods around HCS crossings as solar cycle progresses. They show consistent occurrence patterns indicating they're diagnostics of HCS-related energization.
AI.conclusion: Hammerhead proton distributions serve as diagnostics for energization processes associated with the Heliospheric Current Sheet and its escaping wind, providing important constraints for future simulations.

title: Mixed precision thin SVD algorithms based on the Gram matrix
summary: In this work, we present a mixed precision algorithm that leverages the Gram matrix and Jacobi methods to compute the singular value decomposition (SVD) of tall-and-skinny matrices. By constructing the Gram matrix in higher precision and coupling it with a Jacobi algorithm, our theoretical analysis and numerical experiments both indicate that the singular values computed by this mixed precision thin SVD algorithm attain high relative accuracy. In practice, our mixed precision thin SVD algorithm yields speedups of over 10x on a single CPU and about 2x on distributed memory systems when compared with traditional thin SVD methods.

AI.tldr: Mixed precision algorithm using Gram matrix and Jacobi methods for SVD of tall-and-skinny matrices achieves high relative accuracy with 10x speedup on single CPU and 2x on distributed systems.
AI.motivation: To develop a more efficient SVD algorithm for tall-and-skinny matrices that maintains high accuracy while significantly improving computational performance compared to traditional thin SVD methods.
AI.method: Mixed precision algorithm that constructs the Gram matrix in higher precision and couples it with a Jacobi algorithm to compute the singular value decomposition of tall-and-skinny matrices.
AI.result: The algorithm achieves high relative accuracy in singular values and yields speedups of over 10x on a single CPU and about 2x on distributed memory systems compared to traditional thin SVD methods.
AI.conclusion: The mixed precision thin SVD algorithm using Gram matrix and Jacobi methods provides an efficient and accurate solution for computing SVD of tall-and-skinny matrices with substantial performance improvements.

title: Scattering for Defocusing NLS with Inhomogeneous Nonlinear Damping and Nonlinear Trapping Potential
summary:
We investigate an energy-subcritical defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger equation in $\mathbb R^3$ subject to a lower order nonlinear trapping potential and a spatially dependent nonlinear damping: \begin{equation*}
  i\partial_t u + Δu + i a(x) |u|^{2σ_2} u = |u|^{2σ_1} u + V(x)|u|^{2σ_3} u. \end{equation*} We prove that if the damping acts where $V$ induces concentration effects, i.e. where $V$ is either negative or non-repulsive, solutions are global and uniformly bounded in $H^1$, and scatter in the intercritical regime. A primary challenge arises from the spatial dependence of $a(x)$, which breaks the energy's monotonicity. Consequently, a uniform in time control of the $H^1$ norm of a solution is non-trivial and represents a new result even for $V = 0$. We overcome this issue by introducing a novel energy modified by virial argument, showing simultaneously a uniform bound on the energy and local energy decay estimates, which are subsequently upgraded to scattering via interaction Morawetz estimates.

AI.tldr: The paper proves global existence, uniform H¹ bounds, and scattering for solutions to an energy-subcritical defocusing NLS in ℝ³ with nonlinear damping and trapping potential, overcoming challenges from spatially dependent damping.
AI.motivation: To understand the long-time behavior of solutions to NLS with spatially dependent nonlinear damping and trapping potentials, particularly when damping acts in regions where the potential induces concentration effects.
AI.method: Introduces a novel energy modified by virial argument to overcome the broken energy monotonicity from spatial damping dependence. Uses uniform energy bounds, local energy decay estimates, and interaction Morawetz estimates.
AI.result: Proves solutions are global and uniformly bounded in H¹, and scatter in the intercritical regime when damping acts where the potential V is negative or non-repulsive.
AI.conclusion: The spatially dependent damping can effectively control concentration effects from trapping potentials, enabling global existence and scattering even when traditional energy monotonicity fails.

title: Frequency downshifting stair for ultra-intense femtosecond lasers through a plasma-photonics structure
summary: Wavelength-tunable ultra-intense femtosecond lasers may enable breakthroughs in diverse areas of science spanning attosecond science, particle acceleration and beyond. Conventional crystal-based methods are limited by gain bandwidth and damage thresholds, which restrict their wavelength tunability. Plasma-based frequency conversion, unconstrained by material damage, offers a promising alternative. Here, a novel scheme named Frequency Downshifting Stair (FDS) based on plasma bubble filling control is presented. The FDS enables arbitrary frequency down-conversion of ultra-intense femtosecond pulses and yields chirp-free laser pulses. It can achieve near-100% photon conversion efficiency, approaching the physical limit. This is attributed to the linear control by the FDS of laser chirp evolution during the photon deceleration in the plasma wake bubble. For a laser pulse with an arbitrary wavelength λ_0 (e.g., λ_0=800nm), proof-of-concept PIC simulations demonstrate that a single-stage FDS enables continuous wavelength tuning from λ_0 to {2λ}_0 (800-1600nm). Moreover, a three-stage cascaded FDS achieves more than tenfold frequency (10λ_0) downshifting to a central wavelength of 8.5μm. The FDS scheme thus provides a universal pathway for generating high-energy, few-cycle pulses across the broad infrared regime, offering a powerful new tool for wavelength-dependent ultrafast science.

AI.tldr: Novel plasma-based Frequency Downshifting Stair (FDS) scheme enables arbitrary wavelength tuning of ultra-intense femtosecond lasers with near-100% photon conversion efficiency, overcoming material limitations of conventional crystal methods.
AI.motivation: Conventional crystal-based frequency conversion methods are limited by gain bandwidth and damage thresholds, restricting wavelength tunability for ultra-intense femtosecond lasers needed for breakthroughs in attosecond science, particle acceleration, and other fields.
AI.method: Frequency Downshifting Stair (FDS) scheme based on plasma bubble filling control that enables linear control of laser chirp evolution during photon deceleration in plasma wake bubbles, allowing arbitrary frequency down-conversion while producing chirp-free pulses.
AI.result: Single-stage FDS enables continuous wavelength tuning from λ_0 to 2λ_0 (e.g., 800-1600nm), while three-stage cascaded FDS achieves more than tenfold frequency downshifting (10λ_0) to central wavelength of 8.5μm with near-100% photon conversion efficiency approaching physical limits.
AI.conclusion: FDS provides a universal pathway for generating high-energy, few-cycle pulses across the broad infrared regime, offering a powerful new tool for wavelength-dependent ultrafast science that overcomes material limitations of conventional methods.

title: Reduced Order Model for Broadband Superabsorption of Waves by Metascreens
summary: This work presents a new design for broadband absorption of low-frequency acoustic waves using a thin coating made of subwavelength acoustic resonators arranged periodically on a reflective surface. We first study the associated scattering problem and the corresponding subwavelength resonance problem, and then derive analytical approximations for the resonant frequencies and the reflection coefficient in terms of the periodic capacitance matrix in a half-space with a Dirichlet boundary condition. These approximations yield an effective macroscopic description of the coating via an impedance boundary condition and clarify the mechanism of superabsorption through an approximate coupling condition. Moreover, they lead to a reduced order model that enables efficient evaluation of the scattered waves over a frequency band and accelerates broadband absorption design. Building on this reduced order model, we develop a gradient based shape optimization method using shape derivatives of the resonant quantities to achieve broadband absorption. Numerical experiments demonstrate the broadband performance and the effectiveness of the proposed design procedure.

AI.tldr: A thin coating of subwavelength acoustic resonators achieves broadband low-frequency absorption through shape optimization based on reduced-order modeling.
AI.motivation: To design efficient broadband absorption of low-frequency acoustic waves using thin coatings, overcoming limitations of traditional bulky absorbers.
AI.method: Periodic arrangement of subwavelength acoustic resonators on reflective surface; analytical approximations for resonant frequencies; impedance boundary condition modeling; reduced-order model for efficient evaluation; gradient-based shape optimization using shape derivatives.
AI.result: Derived analytical approximations for resonant frequencies and reflection coefficients; developed effective macroscopic description via impedance boundary condition; created reduced-order model enabling efficient broadband evaluation; demonstrated broadband absorption performance through numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed design using subwavelength resonators with shape optimization achieves effective broadband low-frequency acoustic absorption with thin coatings, validated by numerical experiments.

title: Sublinear elliptic equations with a sharp change of sign in the nonlinearity
summary: We study the semilinear indefinite elliptic problem \[ -Δu = Q_Ω|u|^{p-2}u \quad \text{in } \mathbb{R}^N, \] where $Q_Ω= χ_Ω- χ_{\mathbb{R}^N \setminus Ω}$, $Ω\subset \mathbb{R}^N$ is a bounded smooth subset, $N \geq 3$, and $1 \leq p < 2$, with $p=1$ corresponding to the sign nonlinearity. Using a variational approach, we investigate the uniqueness or multiplicity of nonnegative solutions depending on the shape of $Ω$ and the existence of different types of nodal solutions. We also show that all solutions have compact support and analyze how the support of the ground state depends on $p$, proving convergence to the whole space as $p\to 2^{-}$ and identifying some qualitative features such as starshapedness and Lipschitz regularity of the support. We also establish a link between these problems and a two-phase Serrin-type torsion overdetermined problem.

AI.tldr: Study of semilinear indefinite elliptic problems with sign-changing nonlinearity, focusing on uniqueness/multiplicity of solutions, compact support properties, and connections to overdetermined problems.
AI.motivation: To understand the behavior of solutions to indefinite elliptic problems where the nonlinearity changes sign across domain boundaries, particularly how solution properties depend on domain shape and nonlinearity parameter p.
AI.method: Variational approach to study existence, uniqueness, and multiplicity of nonnegative solutions; analysis of compact support properties and support dependence on p; connection to two-phase Serrin-type torsion overdetermined problems.
AI.result: All solutions have compact support; ground state support converges to whole space as p→2⁻; solutions exhibit starshapedness and Lipschitz regularity of support; existence of different types of nodal solutions depending on domain shape.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes fundamental properties of solutions to indefinite elliptic problems, revealing connections between solution behavior, domain geometry, and nonlinearity parameter, with implications for overdetermined boundary value problems.

title: Quantum lower bounds for simulating fluid dynamics
summary: Developing quantum algorithms to simulate fluid dynamics has become an active area of research, as accelerating fluid simulations could have significant impact in both industry and fundamental science. While many approaches have been proposed for simulating fluid dynamics on quantum computers, it is largely unclear whether these algorithms will provide speedup over existing classical approaches. In this paper we give evidence that quantum computers cannot significantly outperform classical simulations of fluid dynamics in general. We study two models of fluids: the Korteweg-de Vries (KdV) equation, which models shallow water waves, and the incompressible Euler equations, which model ideal, inviscid fluids. We show that any quantum algorithm simulating the KdV equation or the Euler equations for time $T$ requires $Ω(T^2)$ and $e^{Ω(T)}$ copies of the initial state in the worst case, respectively. These lower bounds hold for the task of preparing the final state, and similar bounds hold for history state preparation. We prove the lower bound for the KdV equation by investigating divergence of solitons. For the Euler equations, we show that instabilities enable fast state discrimination.

AI.tldr: Quantum computers cannot significantly outperform classical simulations for fluid dynamics, with lower bounds showing Ω(T²) copies needed for KdV equation and e^Ω(T) copies for Euler equations.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether quantum algorithms for fluid dynamics simulation can provide meaningful speedup over classical approaches, given the active research interest in this area and its potential industrial and scientific impact.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis establishing quantum lower bounds for two fluid models: (1) Korteweg-de Vries (KdV) equation modeling shallow water waves, and (2) incompressible Euler equations modeling ideal inviscid fluids. For KdV, the proof uses divergence of solitons; for Euler, it leverages instabilities for fast state discrimination.
AI.result: Any quantum algorithm simulating KdV equation requires Ω(T²) copies of the initial state in worst case; for Euler equations requires e^Ω(T) copies. These lower bounds apply to final state preparation tasks, with similar bounds for history state preparation.
AI.conclusion: Quantum computers cannot significantly outperform classical simulations of fluid dynamics in general, as evidenced by strong lower bounds on quantum algorithms for fundamental fluid models.

title: Global and local helicity-preservation in the finite element discretisation of magnetic relaxation
summary: Magnetic relaxation drives plasma toward lower-energy equilibria under helicity constraints. In ideal magnetohydrodynamics (MHD), helicity is locally conserved, while resistive theories such as Taylor relaxation preserve only global helicity. This distinction has important implications for structure-preserving numerical methods. We compare three finite element formulations: an unconstrained scheme that does not conserve helicity, a mixed method based on finite element exterior calculus that preserves all local helicities, and a Lagrange multiplier approach that enforces only global helicity conservation. Numerical experiments on braided and knotted magnetic fields show that local helicity preservation prevents spurious reconnection and maintains nontrivial topology in ideal MHD or magneto-friction, whereas enforcing only global helicity allows further relaxation through local reconnection. Numerical results on magnetic knots and braids are provided. These results clarify how different levels of discrete helicity constraints influence magnetic relaxation and equilibrium structure in numerical computation.

AI.tldr: Comparison of three finite element methods for magnetic relaxation: unconstrained (no helicity conservation), locally constrained (preserves all local helicities), and globally constrained (preserves only global helicity). Local helicity preservation prevents spurious reconnection and maintains topology, while global-only allows further relaxation through local reconnection.
AI.motivation: To understand how different levels of discrete helicity constraints influence magnetic relaxation and equilibrium structure in numerical computation, particularly distinguishing between ideal MHD (local helicity conservation) and resistive theories (global helicity conservation).
AI.method: Three finite element formulations: 1) unconstrained scheme without helicity conservation, 2) mixed method based on finite element exterior calculus preserving all local helicities, 3) Lagrange multiplier approach enforcing only global helicity conservation. Numerical experiments on braided and knotted magnetic fields.
AI.result: Local helicity preservation prevents spurious reconnection and maintains nontrivial topology in ideal MHD or magneto-friction, whereas enforcing only global helicity allows further relaxation through local reconnection. Numerical results on magnetic knots and braids demonstrate these differences.
AI.conclusion: The distinction between local and global helicity conservation has important implications for structure-preserving numerical methods. Local helicity constraints are necessary to prevent spurious reconnection and maintain magnetic topology in ideal MHD simulations.

title: Density dependent viscosity for the Poisson-Nernst-Planck-Compressible Navier-Stokes system
summary: This paper is dedicated to the global existence of entropy weak solutions for the Poisson-Nernst-Planck-Compressible Navier-Stokes system in a periodic domain $Π$ d when the shear viscosity $μ$($ρ$) = $μ$ $ρ$ with $μ$ to be constant and $λ$($ρ$) = 0 assuming a pressure state law singular close to vacuum and a $γ$ power elsewhere with $γ$ > 1. It is important to recall that recently D. Marroquin and D. Wang (Arxiv 2024) have proved global existence of weak solutions in the spirit of P.-L. Lions and E. Feireisl when the shear and bulk viscosities are assumed to be constant without singular pressure part close to vacuum namely p($ρ$) = a$ρ$^$γ$ having in hand (from the energy estimate) u $\in$ L 2 (0, T\,; H 1 ($Π$ d )). Here the main difficulty is double first because of the possible degeneracy of the shear viscosity we have to derive a formal mathematical entropy generalizing the BD entropy equality: Up to the authors knowledge this is completely new. With this energy equality and the generalization of the BD entropy, it is then possible to prove global existence of entropy weak solutions for the system. An extra difficulty from a physical point of view remains namely if the density vanishes the compressible Navier-Stokes equations provides no information on the velocity field but the velocity itself is required in the equation related to the concentration. This explain why a singular pressure state law is needed close to vacuum in the spirit of what has been done in different papers see for instance D.

AI.tldr: Global existence of entropy weak solutions for Poisson-Nernst-Planck-Compressible Navier-Stokes system with degenerate shear viscosity and singular pressure near vacuum.
AI.motivation: Previous work by Marroquin and Wang (2024) proved global existence for constant viscosities without singular pressure. Current work addresses the double difficulty of degenerate shear viscosity and the need for singular pressure near vacuum to handle velocity information when density vanishes.
AI.method: Derives a formal mathematical entropy generalizing the BD entropy equality to handle degenerate shear viscosity. Uses singular pressure state law near vacuum (γ-power law elsewhere with γ>1) to maintain velocity information when density vanishes.
AI.result: Proves global existence of entropy weak solutions for the Poisson-Nernst-Planck-Compressible Navier-Stokes system with degenerate shear viscosity μ(ρ)=μρ and λ(ρ)=0 in periodic domain.
AI.conclusion: The combination of generalized BD entropy and singular pressure law enables global existence results for this complex system with degenerate viscosity, addressing both mathematical and physical challenges.

title: Parameter unbounded Uzawa and penalty-splitted accelerated algorithms for frictionless contact problems
summary: We propose a unified iterative framework for the solution of frictionless mechanical contact problems, which relies exclusively on the solution of standard stiffness systems. The framework is built upon a two-step fixed-point algorithm: first, the displacement is computed for given contact forces; second, the contact forces are updated based on the displacement solution. The choice of the dual update scheme depends on the numerical contact formulation under consideration. Specifically, the Uzawa iterative scheme is obtained for the Lagrange multiplier formulation, while a penalty-based operator-splitting strategy is proposed for the penalty contact formulation. The main interest of such displacement-force splitting strategy is to involve only standard rigidity matrices in the solving step: no saddle-point or penalized ill-conditionned coefficient matrices have to be handled. Moreover only the right-hand side of the system is updated throughout the iterations, which enables matrix factorization reuse or efficient iterative solvers initialization. The main limitation of such splitting iterative strategies lies in the inherently slow convergence of the underlying fixed-point iterations. Moreover, convergence is guaranteed only within a narrow range of numerical parameter values (i.e., the augmentation or penalty parameter). This work addresses both issues by applying the Crossed-Secant fixed-point acceleration strategy, which substantially improves the convergence rate and renders the iterative schemes effectively parameter-unconstrained. To the best of our knowledge, this contribution provides the first computational demonstration of efficient, parameter-unbounded convergence for such contact formulations. The substantial practical benefits of the proposed approach are illustrated through representative three-dimensional academic and industrial frictionless contact problems.

AI.tldr: A unified iterative framework for frictionless contact problems using displacement-force splitting with fixed-point acceleration for improved convergence and parameter independence.
AI.motivation: To develop a robust solution framework for mechanical contact problems that avoids handling saddle-point or ill-conditioned penalty matrices, enables matrix factorization reuse, and overcomes the slow convergence and parameter sensitivity limitations of existing iterative methods.
AI.method: Two-step fixed-point algorithm: 1) compute displacement for given contact forces, 2) update contact forces based on displacement. Uses Uzawa scheme for Lagrange multiplier formulation and penalty-based operator-splitting for penalty formulation. Applies Crossed-Secant fixed-point acceleration to improve convergence and remove parameter constraints.
AI.result: Achieves efficient, parameter-unbounded convergence for contact formulations. Demonstrates substantial practical benefits through 3D academic and industrial frictionless contact problems. Provides first computational demonstration of parameter-unconstrained convergence for such contact formulations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses key limitations of existing contact solution methods by combining displacement-force splitting with fixed-point acceleration, resulting in robust, efficient, and parameter-insensitive iterative schemes for frictionless contact problems.

title: Asymptotic behavior of large-amplitude solutions to the Boltzmann equation with soft interactions in $L^p_v L^\infty_x$ spaces
summary: In this paper, we study the global well-posedness of the Boltzmann equation within the $L_{v}^{p}L_{x}^{\infty}$ framework for soft potential models with angular cutoff in a periodic box $\mathbb{T}^3$. By using a time-involved weight function, inspired by the works of [Liu-Yang,2017], [Duan-Yang-Zhao,2013], [Ko-Lee-Park,2022], we overcome the absence of a spectral gap. An analytical difficulty in the $L_v^p L_x^\infty$ setting is that the standard arguments used in [Ko-Lee-Park,2022], [Li,2022] for the nonlinear loss term are no longer applicable when dealing with time integration involving the collision frequency. To resolve this, we introduce a modified solution operator. Furthermore, we control the nonlinear gain term by deriving pointwise estimates bounded by $L_v^p$ and $L_v^\ell$ (for some $\ell <p$) norms. Thanks to the smallness of the initial relative entropy and Grönwall's inequality, we prove the global existence of unique solutions for large-amplitude initial data and obtain a sub-exponential convergence rate toward equilibrium.

AI.tldr: Global well-posedness of Boltzmann equation with soft potentials in L_v^p L_x^∞ framework using time-involved weight to overcome spectral gap absence, achieving sub-exponential convergence to equilibrium.
AI.motivation: Study global well-posedness of Boltzmann equation for soft potential models with angular cutoff in periodic domain. Overcome absence of spectral gap and handle analytical difficulties in L_v^p L_x^∞ setting for large-amplitude initial data.
AI.method: Use time-involved weight function inspired by previous works. Introduce modified solution operator to handle time integration with collision frequency. Control nonlinear gain term via pointwise estimates bounded by L_v^p and L_v^ℓ norms. Apply Grönwall's inequality with small initial relative entropy.
AI.result: Prove global existence of unique solutions for large-amplitude initial data. Obtain sub-exponential convergence rate toward equilibrium.
AI.conclusion: Successfully establish global well-posedness in L_v^p L_x^∞ framework for Boltzmann equation with soft potentials, overcoming spectral gap absence and analytical challenges in nonlinear terms.

title: Ill-Conditioning in Dictionary-Based Dynamic-Equation Learning: A Systems Biology Case Study
summary: Data-driven discovery of governing equations from time-series data provides a powerful framework for understanding complex biological systems. Library-based approaches that use sparse regression over candidate functions have shown considerable promise, but they face a critical challenge when candidate functions become strongly correlated: numerical ill-conditioning. Poor or restricted sampling, together with particular choices of candidate libraries, can produce strong multicollinearity and numerical instability. In such cases, measurement noise may lead to widely different recovered models, obscuring the true underlying dynamics and hindering accurate system identification. Although sparse regularization promotes parsimonious solutions and can partially mitigate conditioning issues, strong correlations may persist, regularization may bias the recovered models, and the regression problem may remain highly sensitive to small perturbations in the data. We present a systematic analysis of how ill-conditioning affects sparse identification of biological dynamics using benchmark models from systems biology. We show that combinations involving as few as two or three terms can already exhibit strong multicollinearity and extremely large condition numbers. We further show that orthogonal polynomial bases do not consistently resolve ill-conditioning and can perform worse than monomial libraries when the data distribution deviates from the weight function associated with the orthogonal basis. Finally, we demonstrate that when data are sampled from distributions aligned with the appropriate weight functions corresponding to the orthogonal basis, numerical conditioning improves, and orthogonal polynomial bases can yield improved model recovery accuracy across two baseline models.

AI.tldr: Sparse regression for equation discovery suffers from ill-conditioning when candidate functions are correlated, especially with limited/noisy data. Orthogonal polynomial bases help only when data distribution matches the basis weight function.
AI.motivation: Library-based sparse regression methods for discovering governing equations from biological time-series data face numerical instability when candidate functions become strongly correlated (multicollinearity). This ill-conditioning causes sensitivity to measurement noise and unreliable model recovery, hindering accurate system identification in biological applications.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of ill-conditioning effects on sparse identification using benchmark systems biology models. Examines how few-term combinations can create strong multicollinearity, evaluates orthogonal polynomial bases versus monomial libraries, and tests performance when data distributions align with orthogonal basis weight functions.
AI.result: Combinations of just 2-3 terms can exhibit strong multicollinearity with extremely large condition numbers. Orthogonal polynomial bases don't consistently resolve ill-conditioning and can perform worse than monomial libraries when data distribution deviates from the basis weight function. When data sampling aligns with appropriate weight functions, conditioning improves and orthogonal bases yield better model recovery accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Ill-conditioning is a critical challenge in sparse equation discovery for biological systems, arising even with few correlated terms. The effectiveness of orthogonal polynomial bases depends on matching data distribution to basis weight functions, highlighting the importance of appropriate data sampling strategies for reliable model recovery.

title: The "good" Boussinesq equation on the half-line: a Riemann-Hilbert approach
summary: We consider the ``good" Boussinesq equation on the half-line. Assuming existence of the solution, we prove that it can be recovered from the solution of a $3\times 3$ Riemann-Hilbert problem that depends only on the initial and boundary values, and whose jump contour consists of twelve half-lines.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the good Boussinesq equation on a half-line, showing that solutions can be recovered from a 3×3 Riemann-Hilbert problem based solely on initial and boundary data.
AI.motivation: To develop an inverse scattering transform method for the good Boussinesq equation on a half-line domain, extending existing techniques from full-line problems to boundary value problems.
AI.method: Uses a Riemann-Hilbert approach: assuming solution existence, constructs a 3×3 Riemann-Hilbert problem whose jump contour consists of twelve half-lines, with data derived only from initial and boundary values.
AI.result: Proves that the solution can be recovered from this Riemann-Hilbert formulation, establishing a connection between the PDE solution and a purely algebraic/analytic problem.
AI.conclusion: Provides a Riemann-Hilbert framework for solving the good Boussinesq equation on half-lines, potentially enabling solution analysis through inverse scattering techniques for boundary value problems.

title: Leveraging higher-order time integration methods for improved computational efficiency in a rainshaft model
summary:
Cloud and precipitation microphysics packages in atmospheric general circulation models typically use first-order time integration methods with a large time step, requiring ad hoc limiters and substepping of the sedimentation scheme to prevent solutions from becoming unstable. We show that in the latest version of Energy Exascale Earth System Model, E3SMv3, the rain microphysics provided by the Predicted Particle Properties (P3) scheme is underresolved in time at the model's default 300s time step. The P3 scheme requires limiters to guarantee stability, but those limiters make large discretization errors more difficult to detect. When the time step of the P3 scheme is reduced to sufficiently capture correct microphysics behavior, wall clock time of the simulation is increased by nearly a factor of 40.
  Instead of reducing the microphysics time step, we recommend using higher-order time integrators based on Runge-Kutta methods, which offer improved solution accuracy at comparable computational costs. A key to obtaining computationally efficient microphysics results is the use of adaptive time stepping, which also eliminates the need for specialized substepping procedures in the sedimentation process. We also analyze individual microphysical processes by extracting inverse timescales from Jacobians of the process rates, which gives insight about the maximum time step each process is able to take while maintaining stability and accuracy, and about how individual processes should be grouped together for most efficient results. The proposed integrators can achieve the accuracy level required to correctly model rain microphysics parameterizations more than 10x faster than the P3 scheme.

AI.tldr: Higher-order Runge-Kutta methods with adaptive time stepping can solve rain microphysics stability issues in E3SMv3 10x faster than current P3 scheme with reduced time steps.
AI.motivation: Current atmospheric models use first-order time integration with large time steps, requiring ad hoc limiters and substepping for stability. In E3SMv3, the P3 rain microphysics scheme is underresolved at default 300s time step, requiring limiters that hide discretization errors. Reducing time step for accuracy increases computational cost by 40x.
AI.method: Propose using higher-order time integrators based on Runge-Kutta methods with adaptive time stepping. Analyze individual microphysical processes by extracting inverse timescales from Jacobians of process rates to determine maximum stable time steps and optimal process grouping.
AI.result: Higher-order integrators with adaptive time stepping can achieve required accuracy for rain microphysics parameterizations more than 10x faster than the P3 scheme, eliminating need for specialized substepping in sedimentation.
AI.conclusion: Instead of reducing microphysics time step, higher-order Runge-Kutta methods with adaptive time stepping provide more accurate and computationally efficient solutions for atmospheric model microphysics, offering 10x speedup over current approaches.

title: Exponential Stability for Maxwell-type Systems Revisited
summary: Considering a two-by-two block operator matrix system of Maxwell type, we present an elementary way of deducing exponential stability under minimal smoothness (and boundedness) requirements of the underlying domains when applications are concerned. The approach is based on resolvent estimates using block operator matrices.

AI.tldr: Elementary approach using block operator matrices to prove exponential stability of Maxwell-type systems under minimal smoothness requirements for domains.
AI.motivation: To provide a simpler, more elementary method for establishing exponential stability of Maxwell-type operator matrix systems that works with minimal smoothness and boundedness assumptions on domains, making it more applicable in practical settings.
AI.method: Uses block operator matrices and resolvent estimates to analyze the system. The approach is described as "elementary" and focuses on deducing stability properties through operator matrix techniques rather than complex analytical methods.
AI.result: Demonstrates that exponential stability can be established under minimal smoothness and boundedness requirements for the underlying domains, making the results more broadly applicable to practical applications.
AI.conclusion: The block operator matrix approach provides an effective and elementary way to prove exponential stability for Maxwell-type systems with relaxed domain regularity assumptions, enhancing applicability to real-world problems.

title: Block operator matrix techniques for stability properties of hyperbolic equations
summary: Inspired by recent developments in the theory of stability results in the context of certain wave type phenomena, we discuss abstract damped hyperbolic type equations given in a block operator matrix form with regards to asymptotic behaviour of their solutions. Under mild conditions on the operators involved we provide criteria establishing strong or semi-uniform stability. In the particular case of Maxwell's equations, these criteria are implied under mild regularity conditions of the underlying domain causing spatial derivative operators satisfy certain compact embedding conditions and rather minimal assumptions on the damping conductivity. These assumptions improve on both regularity as well as on the structural requirements for the conductivity previously available in the literature.

AI.tldr: The paper studies stability of damped hyperbolic equations in block operator form, providing criteria for strong/semi-uniform stability with applications to Maxwell's equations under minimal assumptions.
AI.motivation: Inspired by recent stability theory developments for wave phenomena, the authors aim to analyze asymptotic behavior of solutions to abstract damped hyperbolic equations in block operator matrix form, with particular interest in Maxwell's equations where they seek to improve existing regularity and structural requirements.
AI.method: The authors use abstract operator theory to formulate damped hyperbolic equations in block operator matrix form. They establish stability criteria under mild conditions on the operators involved, focusing on compact embedding conditions for spatial derivative operators and minimal assumptions on damping conductivity.
AI.result: The paper provides criteria for establishing strong or semi-uniform stability of solutions. For Maxwell's equations specifically, these criteria are satisfied under mild domain regularity conditions (causing compact embeddings) and minimal assumptions on damping conductivity, improving both regularity and structural requirements compared to previous literature.
AI.conclusion: The abstract operator approach yields improved stability results for damped hyperbolic equations, particularly for Maxwell's equations where weaker assumptions on domain regularity and conductivity structure suffice to guarantee stability properties.

title: Gradient-robustness in optimization subject to stationary Navier-Stokes equations
comment: 11 pages, submitted to the proceedings of Numerical Mathematics and Advanced Applications ENUMATH 2025
summary: In this article, we discuss gradient robust discretizations for the simulation of non-linear incompressible Navier-Stokes problem and the optimal control of such flow. We consider several formulations of the flow problem that are equivalent for the continuous non-linear forward problem and compare their gradient robust discretization. We will then discuss the influence of the chosen formulation on the adjoint equations needed for gradient computation in the solution of the optimal control problem.

AI.tldr: Analysis of gradient robust discretizations for nonlinear incompressible Navier-Stokes equations and their optimal control, comparing different equivalent formulations and their impact on adjoint equations for gradient computation.
AI.motivation: To develop and analyze gradient robust discretization methods for nonlinear incompressible Navier-Stokes flow simulations and their optimal control problems, addressing how different mathematical formulations affect numerical stability and gradient computation.
AI.method: The paper considers multiple equivalent formulations of the nonlinear incompressible Navier-Stokes problem, compares their gradient robust discretizations, and analyzes how these formulations influence the adjoint equations required for gradient computation in optimal control problems.
AI.result: The study provides comparative analysis of different formulations and their gradient robust discretizations, showing how formulation choice affects the structure and properties of adjoint equations needed for efficient gradient computation in optimal control.
AI.conclusion: The choice of mathematical formulation for nonlinear incompressible Navier-Stokes problems significantly impacts both the gradient robust discretization properties and the structure of adjoint equations, which is crucial for efficient solution of optimal control problems.

title: Fluid-Structure interactions with Navier- and full-slip boundary conditions
summary: We show the existence of weak solutions to the fluid-structure interaction problem of a largely deforming viscoelastic bulk solid with a viscous fluid governed by the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations. In contrast to previous works, the fluid is allowed to slip along the solid boundary; namely, the so called Navier-slip boundary conditions are considered. Such boundary conditions naturally involve the time-changing outer normal of the fluid domain. Hence, their dependence on the varying geometry is one degree higher than in the previously considered no-slip case, which makes it necessary to adjust the concept of weak coupled solutions. Two classes of test functions are introduced: test functions that are continuous over the fluid-solid domain, and fluid-only test functions with nonzero tangential component at the boundary. The weak equations are established until the point of contact, and moreover, compatibility with the strong formulation is shown.

AI.tldr: Weak solutions exist for fluid-structure interaction with Navier-slip boundary conditions, allowing fluid slip at deforming viscoelastic solid boundaries.
AI.motivation: Previous works only considered no-slip boundary conditions where fluid adheres to solid boundaries. This paper addresses the more realistic scenario where fluid can slip along deforming solid boundaries, which introduces higher geometric complexity due to time-changing normal vectors.
AI.method: Introduces two classes of test functions: 1) continuous test functions over the entire fluid-solid domain, and 2) fluid-only test functions with nonzero tangential components at the boundary. Establishes weak equations that account for Navier-slip boundary conditions and varying geometry.
AI.result: Proves existence of weak solutions for the fluid-structure interaction problem with Navier-slip boundary conditions. Shows compatibility with strong formulation and establishes weak equations up to the point of contact.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extends weak solution theory to fluid-structure interaction problems with Navier-slip boundary conditions, overcoming the additional geometric complexity introduced by time-changing normal vectors at deforming boundaries.

title: Operator Splitting, Policy Iteration, and Machine Learning for Stochastic Optimal Control
summary: We propose a splitting approach to solve the second-order Hamilton--Jacobi equation, reducing it to a heat step and a purely first-order step. The latter is implemented using a gradient value policy iteration algorithm, enabling efficient characteristic-based machine learning methods. We establish convergence rates for the splitting method. In particular, the $L^\infty$ error is bounded below by $\mathcal{O}(h)$ and above by $\mathcal{O}(h^{1/7})$ for Lipschitz initial data; this improves to $\mathcal{O}(h^{1/5})$ for semiconcave data and to $\mathcal{O}(h^{1/3})$ for $C^2$ data. We also prove an upper $L^1$ error estimate of order $\mathcal{O}(h^{1/2})$ in the periodic setting, where $h$ is the splitting step. For the first-order step, we provide a weighted $L^2$ error analysis that shows exponential convergence. Each iteration solves linear characteristic equations and learns the value function by minimizing a weighted value gradient loss. The approach yields stable and accurate numerical results.

AI.tldr: A splitting method for second-order Hamilton-Jacobi equations that decomposes the problem into heat and first-order steps, with convergence rates proven for different data regularity and exponential convergence for the first-order step via gradient value policy iteration.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient numerical method for solving second-order Hamilton-Jacobi equations by leveraging splitting techniques and machine learning approaches for improved computational efficiency and accuracy.
AI.method: Splitting approach that reduces the second-order Hamilton-Jacobi equation to a heat step and a purely first-order step. The first-order step uses gradient value policy iteration with characteristic-based machine learning methods, solving linear characteristic equations and learning the value function by minimizing a weighted value gradient loss.
AI.result: Established convergence rates: L∞ error bounded by O(h) to O(h^{1/7}) for Lipschitz data, O(h^{1/5}) for semiconcave data, and O(h^{1/3}) for C² data. Upper L¹ error estimate of O(h^{1/2}) in periodic setting. Weighted L² error analysis shows exponential convergence for the first-order step. The approach yields stable and accurate numerical results.
AI.conclusion: The splitting method combined with gradient value policy iteration provides an effective computational framework for second-order Hamilton-Jacobi equations with proven convergence rates and exponential convergence properties, enabling efficient characteristic-based machine learning solutions.

title: Fractional $p$-caloric functions are Lipschitz
summary: We study the parabolic fractional $p-$Laplace equation $$\p_t u+(-Δ_p)^su = 0$$ in the degenerate range \(2 \leq p < 2/(1-s)\). We show that weak solutions are Lipschitz continuous in space and, if \(p > 1/(1-s)\), also in time. We also prove a comparison principle for both weak and viscosity solutions, and establish the equivalence between the two notions of solution.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the parabolic fractional p-Laplace equation, proving Lipschitz continuity of weak solutions in space (and time under certain conditions), establishing a comparison principle, and showing equivalence between weak and viscosity solutions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand regularity properties and solution concepts for the parabolic fractional p-Laplace equation in the degenerate range, which combines nonlinearity (p-Laplace) with nonlocality (fractional operators). This equation appears in various applications and understanding its solution properties is fundamental.
AI.method: The authors use analytical methods to study weak solutions of the parabolic fractional p-Laplace equation. They employ techniques from partial differential equations, including regularity theory and comparison principles, working within the degenerate range 2 ≤ p < 2/(1-s).
AI.result: Main results: 1) Weak solutions are Lipschitz continuous in space, 2) If p > 1/(1-s), solutions are also Lipschitz continuous in time, 3) A comparison principle holds for both weak and viscosity solutions, 4) Weak and viscosity solutions are equivalent.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes important regularity properties for the parabolic fractional p-Laplace equation and shows that weak and viscosity solution concepts coincide in this setting, providing a solid foundation for further study of this nonlinear nonlocal parabolic equation.

title: Compactness in Dimension Five and Equivariant Noncompactness for the CR Yamabe Problem
summary: We study compactness and noncompactness phenomena for the CR Yamabe equation on compact strictly pseudoconvex CR manifolds. First, in dimension five we establish uniform \emph{a priori} estimates for families of positive solutions of subcritical equations for the conformal CR sub-Laplacian \[ L_{J}u = u^{p}, \] with $p$ bounded away from the critical exponent, assuming positivity of the CR Yamabe constant and positivity of the $p$-mass at every point. As a consequence, the corresponding set of solutions is precompact in Hölder topologies. Secondly, we consider the equivariant CR Yamabe problem for a compact subgroup $G$ of pseudo-Hermitian transformations. We construct a $G$-invariant CR structure on $S^{3}$, not equivalent to the standard one, for which the associated CR Yamabe equation admits a sequence of $G$-invariant solutions whose maxima diverge, thereby proving noncompactness in the equivariant setting. The arguments combine a Pohozaev-type identity in pseudohermitian normal coordinates with a blow-up analysis and Liouville-type classification results on the Heisenberg group.

AI.tldr: This paper studies compactness and noncompactness phenomena for the CR Yamabe equation on compact strictly pseudoconvex CR manifolds, establishing uniform a priori estimates in dimension 5 and constructing examples with diverging solutions in the equivariant setting.
AI.motivation: To understand the compactness properties of solution sets for the CR Yamabe equation, which is important for understanding the existence and behavior of solutions to this geometric PDE on CR manifolds.
AI.method: 1. In dimension 5: Establish uniform a priori estimates for families of positive solutions of subcritical equations using positivity of CR Yamabe constant and p-mass. 2. For equivariant setting: Construct G-invariant CR structure on S^3 not equivalent to standard one, and analyze sequences of G-invariant solutions. Combines Pohozaev-type identity in pseudohermitian normal coordinates with blow-up analysis and Liouville-type classification results on the Heisenberg group.
AI.result: 1. In dimension 5: Solution set is precompact in Hölder topologies under given conditions. 2. For equivariant CR Yamabe problem: Constructed G-invariant CR structure on S^3 with sequence of G-invariant solutions whose maxima diverge, proving noncompactness in equivariant setting.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates both compactness (in dimension 5 under certain conditions) and noncompactness (in equivariant setting) phenomena for the CR Yamabe equation, showing that solution behavior depends on dimension, exponent range, and symmetry considerations.

title: Zonal states and improved $L^\infty$ bounds for eigenfunctions of magnetic Laplacians on hyperbolic surfaces
summary: We establish polynomially improved $L^\infty$ bounds for eigenfunctions of magnetic Laplacians on hyperbolic surfaces in the critical energy regime. We also show that, below the critical energy, the Hörmander bound is saturated by explicit eigenstates, which we call \emph{magnetic zonal states}. These states resemble zonal harmonics on the sphere and equidistribute on Lagrangian tori in phase space.

AI.tldr: Polynomially improved L∞ bounds for magnetic Laplacian eigenfunctions on hyperbolic surfaces at critical energy; below critical energy, Hörmander bound is saturated by magnetic zonal states resembling spherical zonal harmonics.
AI.motivation: To understand eigenfunction behavior of magnetic Laplacians on hyperbolic surfaces, particularly establishing improved bounds at critical energy and characterizing states that saturate existing bounds.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis establishing polynomially improved L∞ bounds for eigenfunctions; construction and analysis of explicit magnetic zonal states below critical energy.
AI.result: 1) Polynomially improved L∞ bounds for eigenfunctions at critical energy; 2) Demonstration that Hörmander bound is saturated by magnetic zonal states below critical energy; 3) These states resemble zonal harmonics and equidistribute on Lagrangian tori in phase space.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides significant improvements to eigenfunction bounds for magnetic Laplacians on hyperbolic surfaces and characterizes the extremal states that saturate classical bounds, revealing connections to spherical harmonics and phase space dynamics.

title: Quantitative homogenization of forced geometric motions through random fields of obstacles
summary:
We establish a quantitative homogenization result for an interface moving through a field of sufficiently sparse but possibly impenetrable random obstacles. From a physical viewpoint, such problems arise e.g. in the context of the motion of dislocations or magnetic domain walls in a material with impurities.
  More precisely, given an interface moving by forced mean curvature flow -- with a positive global driving force plus a spatially fluctuating (negative) driving force modeling the obstacles -- , we prove that the effective large-scale behavior of the forward front is governed by a constant-speed effective motion. For typical values of the global forcing, on large scales of the order $\varepsilon^{-1}$ we obtain a (relative) error estimate for arrival times of the front of the order $\varepsilon^{1/9-}$.
