  Previous stochastic homogenization results for forced mean curvature motion in the literature have required a positive pointwise lower bound on the combined forcing, which implies the absence of any actual obstacles capable of locally blocking the interface motion. In contrast, our results are valid even in the presence of islands with locally negative forcing, potentially allowing for locally pinned interfaces and eventually enclosures left behind the main front. Thus, our homogenization result applies to settings closer to (but still strictly away from) the pinning-depinning transition.

AI.tldr: Quantitative homogenization for interfaces moving through sparse random obstacles, showing effective constant-speed motion with error bounds, even with locally negative forcing that allows pinning.
AI.motivation: Study interface motion through random obstacles (like dislocations or magnetic domain walls with impurities) where previous homogenization required positive forcing everywhere, but real obstacles can locally block motion.
AI.method: Analyze interface moving by forced mean curvature flow with global positive driving force plus spatially fluctuating negative driving force modeling obstacles. Prove homogenization to constant-speed effective motion.
AI.result: Effective large-scale behavior is constant-speed motion. For typical global forcing values, arrival time error bound of order ε^{1/9-} on large scales ε^{-1}. Works even with locally negative forcing allowing pinned interfaces and enclosures.
AI.conclusion: Homogenization applies closer to pinning-depinning transition than previous results, handling sparse obstacles that can locally block motion while maintaining effective constant-speed motion at large scales.

title: On the density of the supremum of nonlinear SPDEs
summary:
We study the one-dimensional stochastic partial differential equation \begin{equation*} \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}(t,x) =
-κ\frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4}(t,x) + ρ\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}(t,x) + b(u(t,x)) + σ(u(t,x))\, \dot W(t,x), \end{equation*} posed on a bounded spatial domain, where $u$ is understood in the random field sense. Depending on the value of $κ$, this equation includes the nonlinear stochastic heat equation with Dirichlet or Neumann boundary conditions, as well as the linearized stochastic Cahn-Hilliard equation with Neumann boundary conditions. We prove that the supremum of the solution admits a density with respect to Lebesgue measure. Our approach is based on Malliavin calculus, and in particular on the version of the Bouleau-Hirsch criterion for suprema developed by Nualart and Vives. One of the main difficulties lies in the analysis of the argmax set of the solution and in showing that the Malliavin derivative is almost surely nondegenerate on this set. As a byproduct of our arguments, we also establish Hölder continuity properties for the Malliavin derivative of the solution as an $L^2-$valued process in the regimes considered in this work.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that the supremum of solutions to a class of 1D stochastic PDEs (including stochastic heat and Cahn-Hilliard equations) has a density with respect to Lebesgue measure using Malliavin calculus.
AI.motivation: To establish regularity properties for the supremum of solutions to stochastic PDEs, which is important for understanding extreme values and probabilistic properties of these solutions. The supremum is a natural quantity of interest in PDE analysis and applications.
AI.method: Uses Malliavin calculus, specifically the Bouleau-Hirsch criterion for suprema developed by Nualart and Vives. The main technical challenges involve analyzing the argmax set of the solution and proving that the Malliavin derivative is almost surely nondegenerate on this set.
AI.result: Proves that the supremum of the solution admits a density with respect to Lebesgue measure. As a byproduct, also establishes Hölder continuity properties for the Malliavin derivative of the solution as an L²-valued process.
AI.conclusion: The supremum of solutions to this class of stochastic PDEs has a density, demonstrating regularity properties. The Malliavin calculus approach successfully handles the technical difficulties related to the argmax set and nondegeneracy conditions.

title: Statistical regularity and linear response of Mather measures for Tonelli Lagrangian systems
summary: We study the statistical regularity of Mather measures associated with $C^1$ perturbations of a Tonelli Lagrangian. When the unperturbed Mather measure is supported on a quasi-periodic torus with a Diophantine frequency, we establish Hölder continuity of the perturbed Mather measure with respect to the perturbation parameter. The Hölder exponent is shown to depend explicitly on the Diophantine index of the frequency. We also discuss the possibility of achieving Lipschitz regularity using KAM theory.

AI.tldr: Mather measures from perturbed Tonelli Lagrangians show Hölder continuity in perturbation parameter when unperturbed measure is on Diophantine quasi-periodic torus, with exponent depending on Diophantine index.
AI.motivation: To understand how Mather measures (minimizing measures in Lagrangian dynamics) behave under perturbations, particularly establishing regularity properties when the unperturbed system has quasi-periodic structure with Diophantine frequencies.
AI.method: Study statistical regularity of Mather measures associated with C¹ perturbations of Tonelli Lagrangians, using properties of quasi-periodic tori with Diophantine frequencies and relating Hölder exponent to Diophantine index.
AI.result: Established Hölder continuity of perturbed Mather measure with respect to perturbation parameter when unperturbed measure is supported on quasi-periodic torus with Diophantine frequency, with explicit dependence of Hölder exponent on Diophantine index.
AI.conclusion: Mather measures exhibit Hölder regularity under perturbations when the base system has Diophantine quasi-periodic structure, and Lipschitz regularity might be achievable using KAM theory.

title: Weak Solutions to the complex Monge-Ampère flows on compact Kähler manifolds : general measures on the right-hand side
summary: We show the existence of a bounded solution to the Cauchy problem for the complex Monge-Ampère flow on a compact Kähler manifold, with the right-hand side of the form $dt \wedge dμ$ where $dμ$ is dominated by a Monge-Ampère measure of a Hölder continuous quasi-plurisubharmonic function. We also prove that for a given semi-positive big from $θ$, the $t$-slice of the solution is locally Hölder continuous on $\rm{Amp(θ)}$ for all $t \in (0, T)$. Next, we prove a comparison principle when $dμ$ is dominated by a Monge-Ampère measure of a bounded quasi-plurisubharmonic function, which implies the uniqueness of the solution.

AI.tldr: Existence, regularity, and uniqueness results for complex Monge-Ampère flow on compact Kähler manifolds with specific right-hand side measures.
AI.motivation: To study the Cauchy problem for complex Monge-Ampère flow with measures dominated by Monge-Ampère measures of quasi-plurisubharmonic functions, extending understanding of these parabolic equations.
AI.method: Analysis of the complex Monge-Ampère flow equation on compact Kähler manifolds, focusing on existence via bounded solutions, regularity via Hölder continuity on ample loci, and uniqueness via comparison principle.
AI.result: 1) Existence of bounded solution for right-hand side dt∧dμ where dμ is dominated by Monge-Ampère measure of Hölder continuous quasi-plurisubharmonic function. 2) Local Hölder continuity of t-slice on Amp(θ) for all t∈(0,T). 3) Uniqueness via comparison principle when dμ is dominated by Monge-Ampère measure of bounded quasi-plurisubharmonic function.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes comprehensive results for complex Monge-Ampère flow: existence, regularity, and uniqueness under specific measure domination conditions, advancing the theory of these parabolic equations on Kähler manifolds.

title: The Euclidean $φ^4_2$ theory as a limit of an inhomogeneous Bose gas
summary: We prove that the grand canonical Gibbs state of an interacting two-dimensional quantum Bose gas confined by a trapping potential converges to the complex Euclidean field theory with local quartic self-interaction, when the density of the gas becomes large and the range of the interaction becomes small. We obtain convergence of the relative partition function and convergence in $L^1 \cap L^\infty$ of the renormalised reduced density matrices. The field theory is supported on distributions of negative regularity, which requires a renormalisation by divergent mass and energy counterterms. Unlike previous results in the homogeneous setting of the torus without a trapping potential, the counterterms are not given by a finite collection of scalars but by diverging counterterm functions. This leads to significant new mathematical challenges. For our proof, we also derive quantitative bounds on the Green function of Schrödinger operators and of its gradient, which might be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: 2D interacting Bose gas Gibbs state converges to complex Euclidean field theory with quartic self-interaction in high-density, short-range limit.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous connection between quantum many-body systems and quantum field theory, extending previous homogeneous results to trapped systems where counterterms become functions rather than scalars.
AI.method: Prove convergence of grand canonical Gibbs state to field theory with renormalization by divergent mass and energy counterterm functions, using quantitative bounds on Schrödinger Green functions and their gradients.
AI.result: Convergence of relative partition function and L¹∩L∞ convergence of renormalised reduced density matrices, with field theory supported on distributions of negative regularity.
AI.conclusion: Trapped 2D Bose gases converge to Euclidean field theory in high-density limit, requiring function-valued counterterms that present new mathematical challenges beyond homogeneous cases.
